excerpts.name: Preface Zhao Mingcheng
excerpts.text:
SKQS edition
金石錄原序 余自少小，喜從當世學士大夫訪問前代金石刻詞，以廣異聞。後得歐陽文忠公集古錄，讀而賢之，以為是正譌謬，有功於後學甚大，惜其尚有漏落，又無嵗月先後之次，思欲廣而成書以傳學者。於是益訪求藏畜，凡二十年而後粗備。上自三代，下訖隋唐五季；内自京師，達于四方，遐邦絶域、夷狄所傳；倉史以来古文竒字、大小二篆、分𨽻、行草之書；鍾鼎、簠簋、尊敦、甗鬲、盤杅之銘；詞人墨客詩歌賦頌、碑誌敘記之文章；名卿賢士之功烈行治；至於浮屠老子之説；凡古物竒器、豐碑巨刻所載，與夫殘章斷畫磨滅而僅存者，畧無遺矣。因次其先後，為二千卷。余之致力於斯，可謂勤且久矣，非特區區為玩好之具而已也。盖竊嘗以謂：詩書以後，君臣行事之迹悉載於史，雖是非褒貶出於秉筆者私意，或失其實；然至其善惡大節，有不可誣；而又傳諸既久，理當依據。若夫嵗月、地理、官爵、世次，以金石刻考之，其牴牾十常三四。盖史牒出於後人之手，不能無失；而刻詞當時所立，可信不疑。則又攷其異同，㕘以他書，為金石錄三十卷。至於文辭之媺惡、字畫之工拙，覽者當自得之，皆不復論。嗚呼！自三代以来，聖賢遺迹著于金石者多矣。盖其風雨侵蝕，與夫樵夫牧童毁傷淪棄之餘，幸而存者止此爾。是金石之固，猶不足恃。然則所謂二千卷者，終歸於磨滅；而余之是書，有時而或傳也。孔子曰：「飽食終日，無所用心，難矣哉！不有博奕者乎？為之猶賢乎已。」是書之成，其賢于無所用心，豈特博奕之比乎？輒錄而傳諸後世，好古博雅之士，其必有補焉。東武趙明誠序。

**Original Preface to the _Records of Metal and Stone_**

From a young age, I enjoyed inquiring among the scholar-officials of my time about inscribed texts on metal and stone from previous eras, in order to broaden my uncommon knowledge. Later, I obtained the _Collection of Ancient Records_ (_Ji Gu Lu_) by Ouyang Xiu [Ouyang Wenzhong Gong], read it, and esteemed it highly. I considered it greatly meritorious for later scholars in correcting errors and falsehoods. However, I regretted that it still had omissions and lacked chronological arrangement by year and month. Thus, I conceived the desire to expand upon it and create a book to transmit to scholars.

Thereupon, I increasingly sought out and gathered [inscriptions], and only after twenty years did I roughly assemble a collection. It ranges from the Three Dynasties [Xia, Shang, Zhou] down until the Sui, Tang, and Five Dynasties; geographically, it extends from the Capital outward to the four directions, [including] remote states and distant regions, and lands inhabited by non-Han peoples. [It includes:] ancient scripts and unusual characters since the time of Cang[jie] and Shi [Huang], Greater and Lesser Seal scripts, Clerical script, and Running and Cursive scripts; inscriptions on bells (zhong), tripods (ding), rectangular containers (fu), round containers (gui), wine vessels (zun, dun), steamers (yan), hollow-legged tripods (li), plates (pan), and basins (yu); the writings of poets and calligraphers – poems, songs, rhapsodies, eulogies, stele inscriptions (bei), epitaphs (zhi), narratives (xu), and records (ji); the meritorious deeds and administrative achievements of famous ministers and worthy gentlemen; even down to the doctrines of Buddhists [浮屠 - Futu] and Daoists [老子 - Laozi]. In essence, everything recorded on ancient objects, strange artifacts, great steles, and massive carvings – including fragmentary passages and broken lines, worn away yet barely surviving – almost nothing has been omitted.

I then arranged these items chronologically, making up two thousand scrolls [referring to the collection of rubbings/items]. My dedication to this task can indeed be called diligent and long-lasting; it was not merely undertaken as a trifling hobby.

For I have ventured to hold the view that: after the _Book of Poetry_ and the _Book of Documents_, the deeds of rulers and ministers are all recorded in the Histories. Although judgments of right and wrong, praise and blame, may stem from the personal biases of the historian and perhaps misrepresent the truth; nevertheless, regarding their major points of good and evil, these cannot be falsified; and moreover, accounts that have been transmitted for a long time should reasonably be relied upon. However, when details such as dates, geographical locations, official titles, and generational sequences are examined by means of metal and stone inscriptions, discrepancies are found in three or four out of every ten cases. This is because historical documents are produced by later hands and cannot be without error, whereas inscribed texts were erected at the time and can be trusted without doubt. Therefore, I examined these discrepancies, consulted other books for comparison, and compiled the _Records of Metal and Stone_ (_Jinshi Lu_) in thirty volumes. As for the literary quality [beauty or ugliness] of the texts or the technical skill [skillfulness or clumsiness] of the calligraphy, viewers must ascertain these for themselves; I do not discuss them further.

Alas! Since the Three Dynasties, many traces left by sages and worthies have been inscribed on metal and stone. What fortunately survives today is merely what remains after the erosion of wind and rain, and the destruction and abandonment by woodcutters and herd boys. Thus, the durability of metal and stone is still not something to be relied upon completely. If that is so, then the aforementioned two thousand scrolls [of collected items] will ultimately perish through wear and tear; but this book of mine may perhaps be transmitted for a time.

Confucius said, "To eat one's fill all day long without applying one's mind is indeed difficult! Are there not games like Bo and Yi? Even playing them is better than doing nothing." The completion of this book, in being better than having nothing to apply the mind to, how can it be compared merely to games like Bo and Yi? I therefore record this and transmit it to later generations; may it surely be of some benefit to antiquarians and broadly learned scholars.

Preface by Zhao Mingcheng of Dongwu.

excerpts.name: SKQS abstract
excerpts.text:
《金石錄》•三十卷〈兩淮馬裕家藏本〉
宋趙明誠撰。明誠字德父，密州諸城人。曆官知湖州軍州事。是書以所藏三代彝器及漢唐以來石刻，仿歐陽修《集古錄》例，編排成帙。紹興中，其妻李清照表上於朝。張端義《貴耳集》謂，清照亦筆削其間，理或然也。有明誠自序並清照後序。前十卷皆以時代為次，自第一至二千鹹著於目。每題下注年月、撰書人名。後二十卷為辨證，凡跋尾五百二篇。中邢義、李證、義興茶舍、般舟和尚四碑，目錄中不列其名。或編次偶有疏舛，或所續得之本未及補入卷中歟？初鋟版於龍舒。開禧元年，浚儀趙不譾又重刻之，其本今已罕傳。故歸有光、朱彝尊所見皆傳抄之本，或遂指為未完之書。其實當時有所考證，乃為題識。故李清照跋稱，二千卷中有題跋者五百二卷耳。原非卷卷有跋，未可以殘闕疑也。清照跋，據洪邁《容齋四筆》，原為龍舒刻本所不載。邁於王順伯家見原稿，乃撮述大概載之。此本所列，乃與邁所撮述者不同，則後人補入，非清照之全文矣。自明以來，轉相抄錄，各以意為更移。或刪除其目內之次第，又或竄亂其目之年月。第十一卷以下，或並削每卷之細目，或竟佚卷末之後序，沿訛踵謬，彌失其真。顧炎武《日知錄》載章邱刻本，至以後序“壯月朔”為“牡丹朔”，其書之舛謬，可以概見。近日所傳，惟焦竑從秘府抄出本、文嘉從宋刻影抄本、昆山葉氏本、閩中徐氏本、濟南謝氏重刻本，又有長洲何焯、錢塘丁敬諸校本，差為完善。今揚州刻本，皆為採錄。又於注中以《隸釋》、《隸續》諸書增附案語，較為詳核。別有範氏天一閣、惠氏紅豆山房諸校本，皆稍不及。故今從揚州所刊著於錄焉。


**_Jinshi Lu_ (Record of Metal and Stone) • Thirty Juan (From the collection of Ma Yu of Lianghuai) 


Authored by Zhao Mingcheng of the Song dynasty. Mingcheng's courtesy name was Defu; he was a native of Zhucheng in Mizhou. He served in office, including as Prefect of Huzhou Military Prefecture. This book takes the ritual vessels of the Three Dynasties (Xia, Shang, Zhou) and the stone inscriptions from the Han and Tang dynasties onwards that [Zhao] collected, follows the precedent of Ouyang Xiu's _Ji Gu Lu_, and arranges them into volumes. During the Shaoxing reign [1131-1162], his wife, Li Qingzhao, submitted it to the court via memorial. Zhang Duanyi's _Gui Er Ji_ (Collection for Noble Ears) states that Qingzhao also made editorial revisions therein; reason suggests this may perhaps be so. It includes Mingcheng's own preface and Qingzhao's postface.

The first ten juan are arranged chronologically; items numbered from one to two thousand are all listed in the table of contents. Below each title, the date (year and month) and the names of the composer and calligrapher are noted. The latter twenty juan contain critical discussions, totaling 502 colophons. Among these, four stelae—[those concerning] Xing Yi, Li Zheng, the Yixing Teahouse, and the Pratyutpanna Monk—are not listed by name in the table of contents. Perhaps there were occasional errors in the compilation order, or perhaps items obtained later were not added into the volumes in time?

It was initially engraved in Longshu. In the first year of the Kaixi reign [1205], Zhao Buchien of Junyi re-engraved it; that version is now rarely transmitted. Therefore, the versions seen by Gui Youguang and Zhu Yizun were all manuscript copies, leading some perhaps to designate it as an incomplete work. In reality, comments were only made at that time when some research had been done. Therefore, Li Qingzhao's colophon states that among the two thousand items, only 502 had colophons. It was not originally the case that every item had a colophon; one cannot suspect it of being incomplete on these grounds.

Qingzhao's colophon, according to Hong Mai's _Rongzhai Sibi_ (Four Jottings from the Tolerant Studio), was originally not included in the Longshu engraved edition. Mai saw the original manuscript at Wang Shunbo's house and then recorded a summary of its general outline. The version [of the postface] listed in this edition differs from Mai's summary; thus, it must have been added by later people and is not Qingzhao's complete text.

Since the Ming dynasty, it has been repeatedly copied, with each copyist making changes according to their own ideas. Some deleted the sequence numbers within the table of contents, while others disordered the dates listed therein. From the eleventh juan onwards, some versions also eliminated the detailed table of contents for each volume, or even lost the postface at the end; following errors and repeating mistakes, the work increasingly lost its original form. Gu Yanwu's _Rizhi Lu_ (Record of Daily Learning) records a Zhangqiu engraved edition which goes so far as to render the postface date "Zhuang Month First Day" (壯月朔 - first day of the 8th lunar month) as "Peony Month First Day" (牡丹朔); the extent of the book's textual corruption can be generally seen from this.

Among recently transmitted versions, only the copy made by Jiao Hong from the Imperial Library, the tracing copy made by Wen Jia from a Song engraving, the Kunshan Ye family version, the Minzhong Xu family version, the Jinan Xie family re-engraved version, and also the collated versions by He Zhuo of Changzhou and Ding Jing of Qiantang are relatively complete and sound. The current Yangzhou engraved edition incorporates material from these versions. Furthermore, within the notes, it adds editorial remarks drawing on works like the _Li Shi_ and _Li Xu_, making it comparatively detailed and reliable. Other collated versions, such as those from the Fan family's Tianyi Ge library and the Hui family's Hongdou Shanfang library, are all slightly inferior.

Therefore, we now follow the Yangzhou engraved edition for this catalog entry.

excerpts.name: SBCK postscript
excerpts.text:
跋 是書宋刻，世間僅存十卷，卽跋尾之卷十一至二十，今藏滂喜齋潘氏，迄未寓目。其傳鈔之善者，推葉文莊本、吳文定本、錢罄室本。葉、吳二本，何義門均獲見之；唯錢本，則僅見陸勅先所過校者。何氏復自有校定之本。盧見曾得之，又得景鈔濟南謝世箕刊本，因刻入雅雨堂叢書。顧千里嘗以葉、錢二氏鈔本覆校盧刻，糾正其訛奪甚多。是爲石門呂无黨手抄，舊藏余家。卷中遇「留」字均缺筆；遇「啓」字、「學」字同。後六卷別出一手，於「留」字外兼避「公」字，蓋亦晚村後裔也。无黨抄筆精整，全書讐校極審愼，然鈔校均不言所據何本。余從鐵琴銅劍樓借顧氏校本讐對。是固遠勝盧刻，與葉、錢二本互有異同，較近錢本，而亦不盡合。意者，所據爲吳文定本耶？滂喜齋藏書記諸家跋文所舉宋本佳處，是本多同；其宋本誤者，此亦誤。惟第十四卷漢從事武梁碑跋字「綏宗體德忠孝」，此「綏宗」下衍「掾」字；第十七卷漢費君碑陰跋「甘陵石」，此作「石勖」。卽顧氏亦未校正，此則稍有瑕疵耳。顧氏兼錄義門校筆，旣正盧刻之譌，其足爲是本借鏡者亦自不尠。今以呂氏原校及顧氏所校與是本互異之處，彙錄校記，附刊於後，庶幾成一善本乎？海鹽張元濟。

**Postscript**

Regarding the Song dynasty engraved edition (宋刻) of this book, only ten volumes survive in the world today, namely volumes eleven to twenty containing the postscripts (跋尾之卷). They are currently held by the Pan family of Pangxi Studio (滂喜齋潘氏), [but] I have not yet had the opportunity to see them. Among the well-regarded manuscript copies (傳鈔), those of Ye Wen Zhuang [Ye Mengde, 葉夢得], Wu Wen Ding [Wu Kuan, 吳寬], and Qian Qing Shi [Qian Fu, 錢福] are considered the best. He Yimen [He Zhuo, 何焯] saw both the Ye and Wu copies. However, concerning the Qian copy, he only saw the version collated by Lu Chixian (陸勅先). He [Yimen] also produced his own collated version. Lu Jianzeng (盧見曾) obtained He's version, and also acquired a traced copy (景鈔) of the edition printed by Xie Shiji (謝世箕) of Jinan, based on which he included the work in his _Collected Works from the Elegant Rain Hall_ (_Yayu Tang Congshu_). Gu Qianli [Gu Guangqi, 顧廣圻] later collated Lu's printed edition (盧刻) against the manuscript copies of Ye and Qian, correcting numerous errors and omissions.

This [copy I am using] is a manuscript hand-copied by Lü Wudang of Shimen [likely Lü Zuqian, 呂祖謙, or related], formerly in my family's collection (舊藏余家). Within the volumes, whenever the character "留" (Liu) is encountered, strokes are omitted [due to taboo]. The same applies to the characters "啓" (Qi) and "學" (Xue). _[Alternative interpretation: The characters "Qi" and "Xue" are treated similarly.]_ The later six volumes were copied by a different hand; besides omitting strokes for "留" (Liu), they also avoid the character "公" (Gong), perhaps indicating [the copyist was] also a descendant of Wancun [likely Lü Zuqian's style name]. Wudang's calligraphy is precise and elegant, and the entire book was collated with extreme care and prudence. However, neither the copying nor the collation notes state which base text was used.

I borrowed Gu [Qianli]'s collated version from the Tieqin Tongjian Lou [library] (鐵琴銅劍樓) for comparison. This [Lü] copy is certainly far superior to Lu [Jianzeng]'s printed edition. Compared with the Ye and Qian manuscript copies, it has both similarities and differences, being relatively closer to the Qian copy, though not entirely matching it. Could it be that the base text used was Wu Wen Ding's copy? Many of the excellent points of the Song edition, mentioned in various colophons cited in the Pangxi Studio catalogue, are shared by this [Lü] copy; errors known to be in the Song edition are also present in this one. However, in Volume 14, in the postscript to the Stele of the Han Dynasty Attendant Wu Liang, the phrase "[Emperor] Suizong embodied virtue, loyalty, and filiality" (綏宗體德忠孝), this copy erroneously inserts the character "掾" (yuan) after "綏宗". In Volume 17, in the postscript to the reverse side of the Stele of the Han Dynasty Gentleman Fei, where it should read "Stone from Ganling" (甘陵石), this copy writes "石勖" (shi xu). Even Gu [Qianli] did not correct this. These, then, are minor flaws.

Gu [Qianli]'s version also recorded He Yimen's collation notes. Since these corrected errors in Lu [Jianzeng]'s printed edition, they are also quite valuable as a reference for [evaluating] this [Lü] copy. Now, I have compiled the differences between Lü's original collation, Gu's collation [incorporating He's notes], and this [Lü] manuscript into collation notes (校記), which are appended hereafter in publication. Perhaps this will result in a truly reliable edition (善本)?

Zhang Yuanji of Haiyan.

---
